data.title: Macy's: Excellent Value Play As Sales Heat Up Again
data.description:
The stock market is off to a very volatile start so far in 2026, but it remains best for investors to keep their heads down and remain focused on the key themes that could shape market trends through the rest of the year. To me, one of the most important trends will be a corrective rotation away from expensive, momentum-driven large-cap tech stocks and back into value-oriented, beaten-down sectors.

Retail is a good example. Yes, it’s true that we’re facing a weaker consumer spending environment and tariff risks. But amid a sea of industry headwinds, several underperforming retailers are actually doing quite well, and to that end, Macy’s (M) recently caught my eye.

The department store space has long struggled with the erosion of mall culture and the prevalence of e-commerce. But amid that backdrop, Macy's has done a terrific job of streamlining its store portfolio and strengthening its assortment. I’m initiating Macy’s at a buy rating.

Macy’s and its Bold New Chapter strategy

Macy’s is a household name that needs no introduction. However, investors who haven’t caught up with this stock in a few years may be surprised to learn how successful some of the turnaround efforts the company is driving have become.

The chart below showcases the company’s current store portfolio. As of the end of fiscal Q3 (the October quarter), Macy's counted 685 total stores in its portfolio. The company is now back in growth mode, opening a net 5 new stores on a y/y basis after several years of consolidation.

In early 2024, Macy’s leadership unveiled the broad strokes of its turnaround plan, which it labeled the “Bold New Chapter.”

This strategy contained three core tenets. The first was to strengthen its core brand, Macy’s (rather than call its subsidiaries “brands,” the company instead uses the term “nameplate.”) As part of this shift, the company aimed to refocus and centralize resources by closing 150 underperforming Macy’s locations. It also aimed to reimagine the store footprint, leaning away from full-service, large-format mall anchor stores and more toward smaller, specialized locations. The company also leaned in on certain brands to improve assortment quality at Macy’s stores, and also prioritized developing partner-owned (licensed locations).

The second tenet of the turnaround plan centered on the company’s non-Macy’s subsidiaries, Bloomingdale’s and Bluemercury, a beauty retailer that Macy’s acquired in 2015. Again here, Macy’s turned to a back-to-basics playbook, focusing on strengthening its core assortment of brands that consumers would have a difficult time finding elsewhere. Management also focused on a better luxury presence across e-commerce.

The results are surprising. There has been no shortage of headlines about how the luxury category is struggling, hurt especially by a waning consumer profile in the world’s largest consumer of luxury goods, China. Yet (as discussed in more detail in the next section), Bloomingdale’s and Bluemercury are indexing higher than the overall company on comp sales. We note as well that the competitive pool is shrinking, with Saks Fifth Avenue recently in January declaring bankruptcy (it’s also the parent of fellow luxury retailer Neiman Marcus). This is notable, especially since Saks had backing from Amazon (AMZN), with Amazon stipulating that in exchange for financing, Saks needed to sell more of its wares on Amazon.com. Amazon has repeatedly tried and failed to break into luxury, and while Saks has fallen, Bloomingdale’s stands strong.

Also along the way, Macy’s has focused on making its balance sheet more capital-light, unloading a chunk of the real estate in its portfolio. The result is that the company has brought itself down to just $2.4 billion in long term debt (or $2.0 billion in net debt, after netting off cash), which is about a ~1.5x leverage ratio against this year’s core EBITDA - well below the typical ~3x threshold that most commercial banks consider to be a leveraged company.

Moreover, most of this debt is long-dated, giving the company plenty of financial flexibility in the near term to focus on reinvesting in building out its store portfolio again without needing to worry about large near-term maturities.

Recovery in comps is an outlier in a struggling industry

Let’s now turn the discussion to the results of Macy’s turnaround and the positive datapoints we can glean from the latest results. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Macy’s revenue in Q3 was flat y/y at $4.91 billion, which substantially beat Wall Street’s expectations of $4.76 billion (-3% y/y). The magnitude of that beat is a reflection of how The Street's consensus is baking in the battered retail landscape, and yet, Macy's is defying expectations with its stronger performance.

Of course, the timing and speed of recent store closures complicate Macy’s sales picture. The cleaner data point to assess the company’s health is its comp sales growth, which measures revenue growth from stores that have been continuously open for more than a year (and excludes the contribution of newly opened and recently closed stores). Overall comps for Macy's in Q3, inclusive of all three brands plus sales from licensed partner-owned stores plus Macy’s own company-owned stores, grew 3.2% y/y in Q3. This improved versus a -1.9% decline in Q2 and a -1.2% decline in Q3, again marking how much of an outlier Macy’s is, as many retail and apparel companies have suffered worsening comps as the macro picture darkened moving across 2025.

The chart below showcases Macy’s performance by brand/nameplate. Notably, all brands saw sales growth: Macy’s was up 2.0%, Bloomingdale’s stood out with 9.0% growth, and Bluemercury achieved 1.1% growth.

Specifically for the core Macy’s brand, CEO Antony Spring’s remarks on the Q3 earnings call detailed several core categories returning to healthy sales growth:

Looking at category performance, we delivered year-over-year improvements across all lines of business: fine jewelry, watches, handbags, men's career, and ready-to-wear outperformed the total Macy's comp, while active categories were softer. The variety of brands and categories we offer speak to our fashion authority and the relevancy in a way we haven't for years, and we are not done. We're committed to bringing Macy's to the consideration set of even more shoppers and our reimagine 125 locations are providing a road map for the future.”

Furthermore, Macy’s sales improvements are also translating to meaningful profit gains. As shown in the chart below, despite the impact of tariffs, Macy’s held its gross margin relatively flat y/y (losing only -20bps y/y) at 39.4%.

Meanwhile, core adjusted EBITDA (which excludes gains from sales in its real estate portfolio) rose 32% y/y to $273 million, or a 5.8% margin that improved 140bps y/y.

Valuation, risks, and key takeaways

The best draw to investing in Macy's is that despite its recent run-up, the stock remains quite cheap. At current share prices near $21, Macy's commands a market cap of $5.64 billion. Adding on the $2.43 billion of debt, net of $447 million of cash, on Macy's latest balance sheet yields an enterprise value of $7.62 billion.

As shown in the chart below, for the full year FY26 (the year for Macy's ending in January 2026), the company has boosted its comp sales guidance to ~flat or +0.5% growth, which is 125bps better than a previous midpoint that called for a -1% y/y comps decline: again, an illustration of healthy performance against a struggling retail industry. It's expecting 7.5-7.7% in core (ex-real estate) adjusted EBITDA, implying $1.64 billion in core adjusted EBITDA.

This positions Macy's valuation at just 4.6x EV/FY26 core adjusted EBITDA. Given strong tariff/margin management, recovering comp sales, a healthy balance sheet, and strong performance in a luxury category that Amazon has repeatedly tried and failed to break into, I consider Macy's to still be trading at a steal. We also shouldn't forget the stock's $0.18 quarterly dividend, which translates to a ~3.4% annual yield at current prices.

Of course, we shouldn't ignore the possible risks here. Added tariffs on Europe may work their way through Macy's supply chain, especially in Bloomingdale's luxury portfolio. Consumer spending could still weaken further and derail Macy's recent progress in comps improvements.

That said, given the cheap valuation, I still think risks are more than priced into the stock. Buy here with confidence.

data.title: Dow Inc: The Incoming Earnings Will Be Key
data.description:
Introduction

I have switched my opinion on Dow many times as the company is going through a tumultuous period for the chemical industry. And with its earnings report coming out in a few days, I want to see what we should be looking at and if it can be a good entry point into the company’s stock to gauge if it is the start of a turnaround for the broad industry.

As a reminder to readers, Dow Inc. (NYSE:DOW) produces various materials science solutions for packaging, infrastructure, and other consumer applications. Right now, the chemical industry is going through a protracted bottom following the post-pandemic shocks, and many in the industry have been coping with the downturn.

Earnings Preview

The current EPS consensus is standing at around -45 cents, and the revenue estimate is at around $9.47B. While the company cannot control chemical prices, they are currently focused on what it can control, its internal cost structure. Dow is undergoing its plan to remove $1B in annual costs by year-end 2026. In the last quarter, the company stated that they are currently ahead of schedule with around $400MM realized in 2025, but I still want the company to accelerate this based on the dire situation in the chemical market. More so, I especially want the European Restructuring to be fast-tracked as they are the higher-cost assets and the EMEAI region has been a cash bleed. These shutdowns are expected to provide a $200MM EBITDA uplift starting in 2026.

I’ll also pay close attention to the Packaging & Specialty Plastics as it is the heartbeat of Dow. Generally, the segment has accounted for around 50% of total sales and the majority of the company’s EBIT. When this segment’s margins compress as they did through 2025, then we can directly see that overall profitability vanishes. Right now, the segment is fighting in a Polyethylene price war as China is currently in a deepening period of overcapacity. The country’s capacity expansion is expected to hit 27% that should continue to force global prices down. Various producers have gone through a January price push and announced price increases of up to 7 cents per pound. Though it will not impact Q4 numbers, I want to see if the company will comment on it and if it will reflect in the guidance, showcasing the potential bottom in the industry.

On the feedstock side, Dow has been focusing more and more of its operations in North America as feedstock is relatively cheaper compared to Europe and Asian based assets, as it uses Ethane and not naphtha, offering better margins. As long as oil prices stay relatively high compared to natural gas, the P&SP segment should remain low-cost. But with Crude staying lower for longer and natural gas currently spiking on a perfect storm of colder weather and low reserves, I want to see what the company will say about its integrated margins.

I also agreed with the company's net-zero cracker project being delayed by 1 or 2 years, with a potential 2028 to 2029 startup. This was part of the company slashing its CapEx by $1B as I target around $2.5B total. This made Dow free up significant cash in the near term and I want to see if management directly reinvests the cash into high-margin de-bottlenecking projects or if it is using it purely to shore up the current dividend. The NOVA litigation windfall of $1.2B could also act as a strong buffer, allowing the company to remain profitable on a cash-flow basis.

Now if the situation has started to revert in favor of Dow, it is possible that we could see a huge earnings beat thanks to the company's high operating leverage. See, Dow can increase production without significantly increasing fixed costs, meaning that as the period of destocking ends, and demand bounces back, this would result in a disproportionately large jump in profit for the chemical company.

Valuation

There isn’t that much to say based on past earnings but the company is currently trading at an EV/EBITDA of 9.95, which is at around the middle of its peers, with LyondellBasell (LYB) being at 10.95 and DuPont (DD) at 7.76. There is potential for EBITDA to recover in late 2026 thanks to the cost-cutting and the closures of European plants. Moat-wise, I do not believe that Dow has a major Moat outside of its cost advantage in North America. It also has a highly flexible asset capabilities, allowing it to substitute a variety of inputs with its production processes. This lets Dow maximize its profitability as it allows the operations to respond better to market fluctuations, and that is why Dow generally has a slight premium over its peers. The dividend has been cut in order to preserve the balance sheet, which I believe was the right move for the company and still has a fairly strong yield at 5.27% based on quarterly distributions of 35 cents per share, which is a fair opportunity cost for investors.

Currently, plants have been running well below capacity in the 60s to low 70s percentile. If demand picks up, Dow will be able to ramp up production to 85%. Meaning that Dow can see a massive increase in annual EBITDA based on the multiplier effect. Dow could also be well-positioned for the AI data center boom with important growth in wire & cable insulation as the world upgrades aging electrical grids. Dow is also expanding its portfolio of specialized adhesives and thermal management materials for EV batteries that will become key. Though in my opinion, considering the current dynamics of the industry, I expect Dow’s revenue to stay relatively flat through the next couple of years.

Despite my flat assumptions, I believe that Dow can still increase earnings during that time, thanks to its operational leverage, and we could see plant capacity utilization as a key profit driver. In a favorable cost environment, the reduced volume could still drive the margin up. Dow could see its operating margin nearing 15% over that time as demand normalizes and the efficiency plan takes hold over the enterprise. Though it is important to note that lack of profitability is cyclical and not structural, as the company is using this period to aggressively lower its break-even point.

Risks & Catalysts

Though Dow cut its dividend to have a one-time reset, a failure to generate positive FCF by mid-2026 could push management to cut the dividend again. This would crater the company's stock again as institutional and income investors would flee the stock.

If China continues to build its chemical capacity despite weak internal demand, then it may continue to dump cheap plastic onto the global market. This would continue to compress margins indefinitely.

On the other hand, the AI infrastructure surge could push the company's P&SP segment to show double-digit volume growth, thus cementing itself as Dow's new high-margin growth engine, decoupled from the sluggish general economy.

If the 5-7 cents/lb increase on polyethylene has been fully implemented and accepted by the market, it might also signal the end of the supply glut, triggering a re-rating of the stock.

Conclusion

The coming earnings release will be crucial for investors as it will potentially help us to decipher where in the cycle the chemical industry is. Dow could be one of the best placed to capture the rebound, but at the moment, I keep my hold rating on the company as there is still strong pricing pressure.

data.title: MYR Group: Expansion Of U.S. Energy Infrastructure Is A Value Driver, But Valuation Is Elevated
data.description:
MYR Group (MYRG) is a leading U.S. contractor specializing in the construction and modernization of power transmission and distribution infrastructure. The investment case is driven by a structurally rising cycle of grid capital expenditures, fueled by data center expansion, electrification, and aging network assets. We believe MYRG offers direct exposure to this trend with attractive medium-term earnings visibility, but we have some valuation concerns. We rate the stock as a hold.

MYR Group’s history and business

MYR Group is one of the biggest specialized contractors in the US energy infrastructure sector. The company is engaged in the construction, modernization, and maintenance of power lines, substations, and transmission and distribution grids, as well as infrastructure for renewable energy and industrial customers. MYR does not own power plants, but it is its crews and equipment that connect generation, grids, and end users. MYR does not own power plants, but it is its crews and equipment that connect generation, grids, and end users.

As a holding company, the modern-day MYR Group comprises dozens of engineering and construction firms, with each of them historically operating in different US areas—and they were not folded into a single corporation at once. Founded in 1891 in New Mexico as a small electrical contracting firm, MYR in the 1990s began steadily acquiring local energy contracting companies across the country. Currently, rather than a single centralized operation, MYR runs a network of subsidiaries that have held on to their brands, regional ties, and engineering crews but use shared capital and standards.

MYR's business is split into two main areas. The first one is Transmission & Distribution (T&D), covering the construction and maintenance of high-voltage power lines, substations, and distribution grids. It represents the company's historical core, with customers mainly including regulated US electric power giants such as Duke Energy, Southern Company, NextEra, and PG&E, and dozens of regional utility-services providers. These companies are required to invest billions of dollars in grid upgrades that aim to prevent overloads, connect renewable energy sources, and protect against fires and storms. MYR is one of the key contractors for these investment programs.

The second segment is Commercial & Industrial (C&I), where MYR builds and maintains electrical infrastructure for private clients: data centers, factories, logistics hubs, oil and gas and ore mining facilities, as well as power plants. This business is less regulated but more cyclical and generates a higher margin. MYR participates in projects of large data center operators, as well as in the construction of substations and transmission lines for new generation capacities.

An important sidenote is that MYR earns money not from running the infrastructure, in contrast, for example, to how generating companies or data center operators do it, but from carrying out the work to expand this infrastructure. So MYR itself does not have huge capital expenditures, a capital-intensive balance sheet, or large depreciation. Instead, it benefits from infrastructure investments made by energy companies, data centers, manufacturers, and others.

The advantage of MYR is that it benefits from the development of the US energy infrastructure as a whole, without excessive exposure to any particular area (whether traditional energy sources, renewable energy sources, or nuclear power). So if demand for any of these energy sources happens to take a dip, MYR will not face a shortfall in revenue, as demand will pivot to other energy sources—and grid infrastructure is more or less the same for them all.

Investment thesis

MYR Group is primarily a bet that capital investment in US energy infrastructure will rise on a national scale with the aim of expanding and modernizing the network. As the main initiators of future capital expenditures, we see power generation and grid companies, data center operators and manufacturing conglomerates.

The Edison Electric Institute (EEI) estimates that capital expenditures for upgrading the electrical grid infrastructure by IOUs will total $1.1 trillion from 2025-2029. This would be up 43% from the 2020-2024 levels. And this only covers investor-owned electric utility companies, or publicly traded ones.

According to EEI, 91 GW of generating capacity is currently under construction, with another 488 GW planned or proposed for construction over the next five years. To put this into perspective, the current capacity is about 1,250 GW. Collectively, the potential buildouts of 579 GW make up 46.3% of the country's existing power system capacity.

Here are the key drivers of these trends: the rapid growth of AI capacity, production reshoring to the US, aging equipment, and extreme weather conditions. The grid is already suffering from inefficiencies. The rising generation and consumption are compounded by outdated equipment, with its lower efficiency, as losses and associated operation and maintenance costs are higher.

A trend is evident here: Over the calendar year 2024, total generation costs fell by 6.2%, while O&M costs rose by 4.7% (page 28). The costs went up at 31 of the 42 companies that put out earnings reports. Most of the infrastructure currently in operation was built in the 1960s and 1970s, and CNBC reported in 2023 that 70% of energy facilities had been in service for more than 25 years, while they have a maximum lifespan of 50 years. The average service time of distribution transformers had exceeded 33 years, while their standard lifespan is probably somewhere about 40 years. Interestingly, what set off the catastrophic fires on the US west coast was malfunctioning and outdated grid infrastructure, with the responsibility lying with electricity generating companies.

In this context, MYR is a rare pure bet on grid infrastructure without any additions of side businesses, such as water or oil pipelines, excessive exposure to renewable energy sources or AI, etc. The company will benefit from the expansion of any type of energy source—it doesn’t matter whether it is fossil fuels (gas, coal), renewables, or uranium - as each of them needs the same grid infrastructure. For 29 straight years, the company has been one of the top 5 US specialized electrical contractors, according to MYR’s slide presentation.

Earnings outlook for MYR Group

Our expectations for the addressable market of the Transmission & Distribution segment are based on Power Insights forecasts, which are also referenced by MYR Group in its corporate slide presentations. These forecasts indicate that from 2024 to 2029, the compound annual growth rate of capital investment in electricity distribution and transmission will be 4.1% and 9.1%, respectively. By analyzing the charts visually, we can suppose that capital expenses on distribution are set to climb by about 5% y/y to ~$84 bln in 2026 and by 2.4% to ~$86 bln in 2027. Expenses on transmission are poised to rise by 15.3% y/y to ~$86.5 bln in 2026 and by 7.5% y/y to ~$93 bln in 2027.

As for the Commercial & Industrial segment, we base our projections on Statifacts' expectations for the dynamics of the US industrial electrification market. The market is anticipated to expand at a compound annual growth rate of 8.1% over the 10-year period from 2024 to 2034.

Historically, MYR's business has shown mixed dynamics relative to the broader market. The peculiarity of MYR's growth is that it has been uneven. Looking over the data for the last 10 years, we can see the following picture: One year the business grows with an average beta of ~2.2x to the market, and the next year it grows at the same pace as the market. From 2020-2024, the cycle of growth with a beta increased to a total of 3 years. This is our assumption.

We attribute this to the practice of revenue recognition: According to 10-K, MYR recognizes revenue as the percentage of completion (mechanical completion) of a project increases. So we can interpret this as the company building up a portfolio of projects and gradually bringing them to completion within the business cycle—and the more projects are nearing completion, the less revenue remains to be recognized. This is one of the reasons why MYR put up a decline in total revenue in 2024 relative to 2023, which management emphasized in its quarterly conference call (while the company reported strong revenue growth of >20% in 2022 and 2023).

We took into account this pattern when modeling revenue, in addition to revenue forecasts. We expect MYR to continue growing without beta in 2026 and accelerate with a beta of 2.2x in 2027. Here’s why there is no beta in 2026:

  • Management has guided for growth of 10%, which is in line with the projected average growth of the addressable market. So, growth is expected to keep pace with the market.
  • They have said in a straightforward way that no major projects are currently about to start and that clients are likely to become more active from 2027, which is why our model for 2027 is more positive.

Based on these assumptions, we are forecasting that MYR Group will earn a revenue of $3.58 bln (+6.6% y/y) in 2025, $3.93 bln (+9.6% y/y) in 2026 and $4.49 bln (+14.4% y/y) in 2027.

Historically, MYR Group's gross margins have remained within a narrow range, with costs holding between 87% and 89% of revenue. This range was only broken in 2017 (91%) and 2023 and 2024 (90% and 91%, respectively). According to management, the reasons were these:

  • In 2023: inflationary pressure, supply chain disruptions, and labor market challenges;
  • In 2024 (as already mentioned above): achievement of mechanical completions on some projects, including green energy; the revenue fell for the same reason (as we described above in this note). Technically, the decline in revenue could have caused the share of fixed costs to climb, which would also have taken a toll on the margin.
  • In 2017: problem projects where profitability fell as a result of revaluations and postponements (actual gross margin missed expectations by almost 2 pp).

We treat these as one-off or irregular factors. Based on management's rhetoric following the last quarter (expectations are for stable margins) and historical data, we project that the cost ratio will hold in the middle of the historical range of 87%-89%.

SG&A expenses held stable in general, averaging around 7% of revenue—and we are modeling MYR's financial metrics on the assumption that they will remain at this level.

Based on this, we are forecasting that MYR Group’s EBITDA will total $235.1 mln (+97.1% y/y) in 2025, $283.5 mln (+20.6% y/y) in 2026 and $320.6 mln (+13.1% y/y) in 2027. It is important to note that we prefer to calculate EBITDA as operating income + D&A.

Risks that can shatter the investment case

Changes in the market

MYR Group is a beneficiary of some major trends in the US (growth in electricity generation, investments in the expansion and modernization of energy infrastructure, replacement of outdated equipment, etc.). If these trends reverse as a result of, for example, a significant economic downturn, a sharp drop in demand for data center infrastructure, or changing strategic priorities of the government and businesses, the company could face a slowdown or decline in its order book.

Contract-related risks

MYR Group recognizes revenue as the percentage of completion of each specific project increases, and it does the same for the costs incurred to complete that project. The case of 2017 shows that force majeure events can cause delays and bump up the prices charged by contractors, leading to a revaluation of expenses. This, in turn, affects profit and EBITDA.

Valuation

We value MYR Group at $208.3 a share and assign it a HOLD rating. We have calculated the target price based on a projected EBITDA over the forward twelve months and a target EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.9х. We calculate target EV/EBITDA using the historical average base EV/EBITDA multiplied by the projected company's FWD EBITDA growth rate. We define base EV/EBITDA as EV/EBITDA multiple adjusted for (divided by) EBITDA growth rate.

Conclusion

MYR Group remains well-positioned to benefit from the multi-year expansion and modernization of the U.S. power grid. The company’s asset-light business model, diversified customer base, and strong execution track record support earnings growth. As a result, we believe the investment thesis remains intact and justified by underlying structural demand. The only issue is the elevated valuation at the moment, as we believe the fair EV/EBITDA multiple to be lower, so we give MYRG a hold rating.

data.title: Paycom Software: Expected Reacceleration After Tough Years
data.description:
Introduction

The first time I bought Paycom Software (PAYC) was in 2019 and even this first buy is in the red after more than six years. Comparing the performance of PAYC to the stock market development over the same time frame, the development of PAYC was disappointing to say the least. Over the last twelve months, the stock of PAYC fell by about 30%. With this article, I try to explain why I think that the company can get back on track when it comes to stock performance as soon as the results for the fourth quarter are released, and an initial guidance for 2026 will be provided by the management. In the following article, I want to lay out why I think the stock price of the company can significantly increase again with a combination of reacceleration of growth and valuation expansion. After a short description of the company and the competitive landscape, I will explain the latest quarterly results. It is followed by an overview of all the factors which led to the underperformance over the last couple of years and I also take a look at the capital allocation of the company. In the end, I take a look at potential risks and the valuation of the PAYC before concluding the article.

Paycom Software is a Human Capital Management (HCM) software provider and competes with a lot of different companies. The biggest company in the HCM market is Automatic Data Processing (ADP). Other competitors are Paychex (PAYX), Paylocity (PCTY), Workday (WDAY), SAP (SAP) or Oracle (ORCL). They all are either focused on the HCM market or HCM solutions are part of their business offerings to companies – this is especially true for WDAY, SAP and ORCL. Those three companies have their main focus other areas like business software in general. With fiscal year 2024 revenue of $1.9 billion, PAYC is a smaller player in this crowded market. ADP is ten times the size of PAYC with revenue of $20.6 billion in fiscal year 2025 (which ended in June 2025). But PAYC has a unique selling point which is the fact that all services it provides are within one software solution. Whether it is talent acquisition, payroll, time and labor management or human resource management in general, it is all integrated in one software program.

Like man HCM solution providers, the software PAYC is offering is also cloud-based which is the standard today. The main part of the business PAYC has is with small and mid-sized companies in the United States. The company sees an opportunity for growth and market share expansion by targeting larger clients and offering its solution in more countries. In 2023, the company expanded to Canada and Mexico and in 2024, it expanded to the UK and Ireland. With currently more than 37,000 customers, Paycom Software has a lot of different customers and is not dependent on a few customers.

Q3/2025 Results

On the 5th of November, PAYC released its third quarter 2025 results. Revenue increased by 9% to $493 million, and EPS increased almost 50% to $1.91. This jump in EPS was caused by higher other income in comparison to Q3/2024. Excluding this effect, the earnings growth was roughly in line with revenue growth. For the first nine months, revenue increased by almost 9% to $1.5 billion and EPS decreased by 13% to $6.02. The lower EPS was due to a special effect in 2024. The forfeiture of 1.6 million shares of restricted stock granted to Chad Richison in the years before 2024, the company had to reverse expenses it charged in prior years and recorded comprehensive income of $117.5 million.

In the first nine months, the company repurchased shares in the amount of $219 million in comparison to $123 million in 2024. The share repurchases are only offsetting the dilution by share-based compensation and are therefore not contributing to EPS growth. The share-based compensation was about $1.6 per share or about 6% of revenue in the first nine month. In my opinion this amount of share-based compensation is not excessive but significant.

The guidance for the fiscal year 2025 was maintained with an estimated revenue between $2.045 billion and $2.055 billion. This would represent an increase of about 9% in comparison to 2024 and a slight decrease of revenue growth in comparison to the growth in 2024 (11%). In the next section I will touch on this topic again because it is one of the reasons why the stock price fell over the last couple of years.

Reasons for underperformance

The AI threat

Software companies are not benefitting from the current AI boom when it comes to valuation and investor sentiment. The fear that AI might reduce the intensity a certain software is used or eliminate the need altogether keeps stock prices of software companies in check right now. PAYC and other HCM solution provider are in the same group because they offer software solutions regarding human resources. In addition, this software solutions might be also used to a lesser extent if a company is gaining efficiency and needs fewer people for the same workload. With fewer people working for a company, HCM solution provider can’t charge the same amount of money because most payment methods are depending on the number of employees which are working for a company. I won’t make a prediction here whether AI will lead to lower employment in general or if AI will replace certain parts of software solutions. But in my opinion when it comes to HCM software solutions, I can’t imagine that AI will make this software redundant. AI can support the HCM solution and make processes more efficient. If any HCM solution provider can integrate this in its solution properly and offer an even better solution to the customer, it can offset any potential reduction in labor force. Since PAYC offers all its products within one software solution, it has all data in one source and might therefore be able to use those data better in order to train AI models to help improve efficiencies for customers. To sum it up, I don’t see AI as an existential threat to software companies or HCM solution providers in general, but if the sentiment of the market in general doesn’t change, any valuation discount because of this fear might continue to weigh on the stock price of PAYC.

General underperformance of HCM market companies

If the threat by AI wasn’t enough, the market of HCM solution providers suffered additionally due to a slowing labor market in the US. Over the last two years, the number of jobs which are added by companies per month decreased significantly in the US. If the companies are keeping the level of employees steady instead of increasing it, even if the economy expands, the market for HCM solutions providers is not growing the same way it could if more people would be hired. Since part of the price HCM solution provider can charge is depending on the number of employees a customer has, price increases are limited to inflation adjustments and new product offerings if no new employees are onboarded. In my opinion, this is an additional problem HCM solution providers are currently facing and is part of the reason for the underperformance of the stock prices. I already mentioned the almost 30% drop of PAYC over the last twelve months. Looking at the other HCM solution providers I mentioned in the introduction, it shows a similar picture: ADP fell by about 12%, PAYX fell by about 24%, PCTY fell by about 30%, WDAY fell by about 25% and SAP fell by about 11%. ORCL is the only company where the stock price increased over the last twelve months (+20%). But since ORCL is currently transforming to a data center provider for AI applications, HCM will only play a minor role in the business offering of ORCL I assume. Therefore I will skip ORCL in future comparisons in this article.

The slow down of the labor market in the US will affect all HCM solution providers negatively and slow down their revenue growth. This will intensify competition in my opinion and the companies need to offer innovative solutions to their customers to be able to generate more revenue. But since HCM is not limited to the US, the companies can expand to other regions and countries and benefit there from increased labor markets. Since PAYC in particular is currently heavily focused on the US market, the company has a high market opportunity from my point of view to expand beyond the countries it currently covers. In addition, as soon as the labor market in the US reaccelerates, all HCM solution providers are benefitting if their clients are onboarding new employees and increase their headcount.

Slow down of growth

The last topic I want to mention as a reason for the current underperformance is specific to Paycom Software. In 2021, it introduced BETI as an application within its software solution. With BETI, employees can check their own payroll before the payment is made and can eliminate errors. The quality of payrolls increases and reduces the need for corrections and reruns. But since PAYC gets also paid for services like payroll corrections and unscheduled runs, these services are used less frequently because of BETI which leads to less revenue for PAYC. This drag on revenue was particular evident in 2023 but was still an issue in 2025 from my point of view. The onboarding of all clients to use BETI and generate efficiency gains takes time from my point of view. But since most of the clients have switched by now, this headwind should be gone. The company can focus its efforts on new clients, new innovations and revenue growth. In my opinion, the company should be able to reaccelerate revenue growth starting in 2026. At Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference, the CEO Chad Richison made some comments which from my point of view sounded like an expected acceleration of revenue growth in 2026 and beyond. The official guidance for 2026 will be introduced with the results of Q4/2025 at the beginning of February. In case of an acceleration in revenue growth, the company could be rerated by the market and experience a higher valuation which would lead to a stock price increase.

Capital allocation

To get a complete picture of a company it is always useful to take a look at capital allocation. For a company which offers software-as-a-service, PAYC has very little investments to make and is posting high free cash flow margins on a regular basis. Looking at the last three years, the company posted free cash flow (deducting purchases of intangible assets and purchases of property and equipment from cash provided by operating activities) of $228 million, $284 million and $337 million in 2022, 2023 and 2024 respectively. In relation to revenues, we get a free cash flow margin of 17%, 17% and 18% for the three mentioned years. Cash flow was negatively influenced by the expansion of the headquarter of PAYC in 2023 and 2024. In 2025, capital expenditure will also be higher due to the investment in an own AI data center for approximately $100 million. Even with these investments made, the company is generating a lot of free cash flow.

Since the company has no debt and a cash position of $375 million at the end of Q3/2025, free cash flow can be completely used for shareholder returns. The company initiated a dividend in 2023 and currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.375 per share. The dividend was not raised since the launch and it is not clear whether the company wants to follow a dividend growth strategy in the future or keeps the dividend flat for the foreseeable future. In 2024, the dividend consumed about 25% of the free cash flow. The company is also repurchasing shares as already mentioned. The amount varies over time and was between $95 million and $287 million between 2022 and 2024. At the end of Q3/2025, the company has already bought back shares in the amount of $219 million.

Due to the fact that the stock price is on a multi-year low, the company should aggressively buy back its own stock and take advantage of the low valuation in my opinion. I am not sure why a dividend payment was initiated in 2023, but keeping the dividend flat for the time being increases the remaining cash flow which can be used for buybacks. This should support the stock price.

Risks

Before taking a look at the valuation of the company, I want to highlight some risks the company could face.

The first risk is obvious a lack of growth. In case the revenue growth does not reaccelerate or slows down even further, the company could be valued even lower and the stock price could further drop.

The second risk is cybersecurity. For HCM solution provider it is always very important to keep the data of their clients safe. Any data breach would result in a lost of trust and could harm future business significantly. From my point of view PAYC has an advantage on this topic. Since it has only one software application for all features it is offering, it is easier for the IT of the company to keep the data safe and protect the company from cyber criminals.

The third risk is about AI. I mentioned this topic already in this article and it is a risk I want to mention in this section as well. Efficiency gains by customers and maybe HCM solutions which are created by AI could harm future business growth of PAYC.

The last risk that should be mentioned is competition in general. If the company falls behind with innovations and competitors can attract customers with better solutions, revenue growth could disappear altogether or even worse, revenue could decrease. In order to avoid this, Paycom Software must keep innovating to stay on top in terms of innovations.

Valuation

The valuation of PAYC is a very interesting topic I want to take a deeper look in this last section before the summary. I will compare PAYC to ADP, PAYX and PCTY since these three companies are the closest competitors and pure plays in HCM solutions as well. Among those four companies, PAYC has the lowest forward-looking Price-Earning-Ratio (P/E) on a GAAP basis with about 19 as of this writing. PAYX with a P/E of 22, ADP with a P/E of 24 and PCTY with a P/E of close to 32 are all well above the valuation of PAYC. The forward-looking revenue growth is about 6% for ADP, 9% for PAYX and about 10% for both PAYC and PCTY. Comparing the net income margin, PAYC is second only to PAYX. With all these facts together, PAYC is undervalued in comparison to its closest competitors from my point of view. A revaluation of PAYC back to a level ADP and PAYX are trading, the stock price can easily increase by 20%. If the whole industry of HCM solution providers receives a positive investor sentiment, a higher multiple would lead to even further gains for PAYC.

Summary

Paycom Software is a great business in my opinion which is currently punished by the market. The invention of BETI led to a partial self-destruction of its business which hampered revenue growth in the last couple of years. In combination with a loss of trust for software companies in general due to AI, the share price of the company suffered heavily over the last couple of years. But from my perspective, the decline in stock price is overdone, visible with the valuation which is too low in comparison to its competitors. If the company can reaccelerate revenue growth, it may surprise the market and lead to a sharp correction of its valuation. This is the reason I wanted to publish this article before the company discloses its results for Q4/2025 and initiates guidance for 2026. I see a chance for a positive surprise.

data.title: Hang Seng Index: Dropped 1.5%, But Bullish Trend Intact With USD Weakness As A Tailwind
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

Since the start of the week, Hong Kong’s Hang Seng Index has staged a decline of 1.5%, negatively impacted by localized macro headwinds.

Mixed tier-one economic data from China was released on Monday, January 19. Industrial production rose by 5.2% y/y in December 2025, accelerating from a 4.8% rise in November and surpassing expectations of 5% - its fastest increase since September.

In contrast, retail sales rose modestly by only 0.9% year-over-year (y/y) in December, following a 1.3% increase in November and coming in below expectations of a 1.2% increase. It marked the weakest growth since December 2022, despite one of the top economic priorities being pushed by top Chinese policymakers to increase consumer demand.

Hang Seng Index continues to outperform S&P 500, Nasdaq 100, and DJIA

Overall, despite the current week-to-date loss of 1.5% as of Thursday, January 22, the Hang Seng Index recorded a gain of 3.7% and still managed to beat three of the major US benchmark stock indices: Dow Jones Industrial Average (+2.1%), S&P 500 (+0.4%), and Nasdaq 100 (+0.3%), except for US small-cap Russell 2000 that ranked the second spot with a rally of 8.7% (see Fig 1.)

A stronger yuan (weaker USD) provides a significant tailwind

The offshore Chinese yuan (CNH) has appreciated significantly against the US dollar since the start of the new year. The USD/CNH broke below a former key medium-term support of 6.9710 (the September 26, 2024 swing low) and traded to a 32-month low of 6.9595 at the time of writing, supported by the continuation of the 2-year yield premium shrinkage of the US Treasuries over Chinese government bonds (see Fig. 2).

These observations suggest that the USD/CNH is likely to have transitioned into a potential major downtrend phase, which may suggest further US dollar weakness ahead towards 6.8960 per USD in the first step.

Based on intermarket analysis, since December 2, 2025, the inverse of USD/CNH (CNH per USD) has moved in a significant direct correlation with several Asia Pacific stock markets (Hang Seng Index, Hang Seng China Enterprises Index, Shanghai Stock Exchange Composite Index, MSCI Asia Pacific ex Japan), and MSCI Emerging Markets ex China (see Fig. 3).

The recent rise in the offshore yuan (CNH) has led to similar upside movements seen in the stock markets. Therefore, a further strengthening of the CNH against the US dollar is likely to create a positive feedback loop back into the Hang Seng Index.

Let's now decipher the short-term (1-3 days) trajectory of the Hang Seng Index from a technical analysis perspective.

Hang Seng Index reached an inflection zone for a potential bullish reversal

The recent 3.4% decline (high to low) from January 16, 2026 to January 20, 2026 of the Hong Kong 33 CFD index (a proxy of the Hang Seng Index futures) is likely to reach an inflection zone to kickstart a potential bullish reversal and potentially transform into an impulsive up-move sequence.

The price actions of the Hong Kong 33 CFD index have managed to hold at its 20-day moving average, which confluences with the minor ascending channel support from the December 16, 2025 low of 25,087.

In addition, the hourly RSI momentum indicator has staged a bullish breakout, which suggests a revival of short-term bullish momentum.

Watch the 26,330/26,250 short-term pivotal support, and a clearance above 26,870 may set sight on the next intermediate resistance at 27,175 before the major resistance comes in at 27,500 (the long-term secular descending trendline from the January 29, 2018 all-time high) (see Fig. 4).

On the other hand, failure to hold at 26,250 and an hourly close below it jeopardizes the bullish tone for a minor corrective decline towards the next intermediate support at 26,045/5,970 (also the 50-day moving average) (see Fig. 5).

Original Post

data.title: Pebblebrook Hotel Trust: Buybacks, Discount, And 8% Yield With Preferreds
data.description:
While Pebblebrook Hotel Trust (PEB) does not pay out a significant level of income to its common shareholders, the internally managed REIT's portfolio of high-quality hospitality assets, the current depth of preferreds coverage, and a deleveraging balance sheet all open up the company as a healthy long-term investment at its current level. I have a position in the 5.70% Series H Preferreds (PEB.PR.H) and view this as a core position within my fixed-income portfolio. They're roughly flat from when I last covered PEB, with their $1.425 per share annual coupon driving a roughly 8% current yield. These are now swapping hands for roughly $18 per share, an 28% discount to their $25 per share liquidation value, or for 72 cents on the dollar. The REIT was rated non-investment grade at "B" by S&P Global Ratings in 2024, but has since worked to reduce its debt and improve EBITDA in the aftermath of the pandemic.

While PEB has moved up 68% from its 52-week low, the REIT is down 11% over the last 1-year and has a negative return profile looking further out. Hotel REITs are broadly currently trading at the narrowest multiple to funds from operations ("FFO") versus their REIT peers. The opportunity here is for PEB shareholders to be anchored to a recovery of its multiple to FFO and a possible reinstatement of the common share dividends. The REIT currently just pays out a peppercorn per share dividend payment of 1 cent every quarter, down markedly from just before the pandemic.

Stock Buybacks, EBITDA Growth, And Debt Maturities

PEB has been aggressively buying back both its common and preferreds stock, with $1.4 million shares of its preferreds repurchased across its universe of preferreds during the third quarter. This was at an average 27% discount to their $25 per share liquidation values. The REIT's board also approved a new $150 million common share repurchase program, having bought back millions of shares over the last three years, with its weighted average number of common shares outstanding ending the third quarter at 117,555,628. This was down materially from its year-ago quarter. PEB's shares outstanding is down 13.5% over the last 5 years, with the positive cadence with buybacks set to be supported by a cash, cash equivalents, and restricted position that ended the third quarter at $232 million.

The REIT generated fiscal 2025 third-quarter revenue of $398.72 million, a dip of 1.4% over its year-ago comp but a beat by $1.88 million on consensus estimates. This dip was partially led by a same-property total RevPAR that fell 1.5% versus its year-ago comp to $362.12, even as occupancy increased by nearly 190 basis points to 79.9% from 78% a year ago. PEB's same-property average daily rate ("ADR") fell 5.4% to $290.25 from $306.78, with the weakness across these core metrics mainly driven by Los Angeles and Washington, D.C. The dip in ADR was led by what management highlighted as competitive pricing sparked by disruption related to the ICE activity and the National Guard deployments in these markets.

Since the pandemic, the REIT has been focused on orienting its portfolio towards larger, leisure-focused resorts with healthy growth potential. This has been to reduce dependence on business transient travel still in recovery. PEB's leisure revenues have grown to 50% of its customer mix, with a $68 million hotel EBITDA upside opportunity also pending as of the end of the third quarter. The REIT is guiding for a full year 2025 same-property hotel EBITDA in the range of $343 million to $352 million, with an adjusted FFO per share of $1.50 to $1.57. This would place its multiple to the midpoint of this range at 8.06x.

PEB expects $16 million in further EBITDA gains as LaPlaya Beach Resort & Club in Naples, Florida, moves to full reopening following the impact of Hurricane Milton in 2024. There could be another $10 million in EBITDA seen from ROI from recent redevelopments and another $42 million from a continued recovery in urban markets. Crucially, the REIT's balance sheet faces $350 million in maturing debt this year, with around $360 million due for repayment next year. PEB should be able to refinance the bulk of these, with the share repurchase authorization reflective of management's confidence in their ability to manage their liquidity balance against this wall of maturities.

Conclusion

PEB faces risk from a broader economic picture defined by still recovering urban markets, negative economic forecasts, and elevated inflationary pressures. However, the preferreds are being actively bought back, are paying out a healthy 8% dividend yield, and trading at a 28% discount to their liquidation value per share. This has ramped up their attractiveness and means I continue to rate them as a buy, with the commons also rated as a buy at its current multiple.

data.title: 3 Calls For EU Sectors In 2026: Tech Outperformance, Manufacturing Growth Gaps, Small Consumer Gains
data.description:
By Thijs Geijer, Senior Sector Economist, Food & Agri | Maurice van Sante, Senior Economist Construction & Team Lead Sectors | Edse Dantuma, Senior Sector Economist, Industry and Healthcare | Rico Luman, Senior Sector Economist, Transport and Logistics | Katinka Jongkind, Senior Economist, Services and Leisure | Diederik Stadig, Healthcare & Technology Economist

Positive growth for major sectors in 2026

1. Technology is an outperformer, compared to other EU sectors

Production growth in the tech sector is expanding rapidly at around 4.5% and clearly outpaces other sectors. Organisations keep investing in their digital capabilities with continued double-digit growth in IT spending, including on data centre systems such as AI-optimised servers.

Growth in the tech sector is also supported by a range of tech-related incentives at the EU level, such as Horizon Europe/EIC. However, overall growth for the industry lags the US and China. Investment growth for data centres, for instance, shows that the EU is not able to keep up the pace.

2. Mind the growth gap between heavy and high-tech industries

In EU manufacturing, 2026 is expected to be the second consecutive year of production growth, despite the ongoing headwinds for the energy-intensive industry. In response to the US tariffs, tariff pass-through by EU exporters to US import prices is almost 100%. So, US companies and consumers are paying almost all direct tariff costs. Furthermore, EU companies started shifting some of their sourcing and production to local US factories and increased focus on other export markets.

The main contribution to growth in manufacturing is set to come more from high-tech sub-sectors profiting from investments in defence, AI and electrification, as well as from tech-driven industries like biotech, aerospace and pharmaceuticals. The EU’s Recovery and Resilience Fund (RRF) will continue to stimulate investments in the manufacturing sector.

For manufacturers of building materials, it’s a positive trend that the EU construction sector shifts from stagnation to growth in 2026. Prospects for residential construction look more promising, with the issuance of new permits growing steadily. Output in the infrastructure segment will be boosted by a final series of grants and loans out of the EU’s RRF, which still holds over €200 billion in non-committed funds. Besides that, we’ll likely see the first projects funded by Germany’s €500bn investment plan for infrastructure and climate by the end of 2026. Modest growth in manufacturing and construction is a positive signal for the staffing industry, for which we anticipate a gradual improvement in demand for temporary workers in 2026.

The competitive landscape for manufacturing will continue to be very volatile in 2026. Renewed trade tensions can quickly reduce competitiveness. The positive impulse from efforts to diversify trade, such as the Free Trade Agreements with Mercosur and India will only materialise over time. Meanwhile, the implementation of EU policies (and the possibility of last-minute changes) is another major factor influencing the competitive environment. This includes the further roll-out of the Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) and measures on non-EU steel imports.

3. Consumer-oriented sectors: expect marginal gains rather than big improvements

High inflation rates have turned shoppers into savers. Further improvements in purchasing power and a minor drop in inflation in the EU are favourable for consumer-oriented sectors in 2026. Companies are eagerly awaiting such a turnaround, given that the lack of demand is a major concern for many companies, including those in food manufacturing. That’s not surprising if you consider that sales volumes in EU food retail are only growing at a modest pace, and sales volumes in food service are trailing behind.

For non-food retail, there could be an uplift in categories like electronics and furniture as we move further away from the pandemic, when consumers overpurchased on (semi-) durable goods. One thing that consumers and business travellers do spend more on is air travel, which lifts the growth rate for aviation above the average for the transport and logistics sector.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

Original Post

data.title: Top Oil Companies To Invest In For An Oil Sector Revival
data.description:
With the energy sector back in headlines, these are several oilfield services companies and refiners to watch in 2026.

2026 Outlook for Oil Stocks

Energy markets enter 2026 with a mix of near-term uncertainty and longer-term structural opportunity across multiple segments of the oil value chain. While global crude markets remain well supplied, keeping pressure on prices and reinforcing capital discipline among producers, both oilfield services companies and refiners stand to benefit from distinct, and in some cases complementary, dynamics.

For oilfield services providers, the opportunity is tied to the need for maintenance, rehabilitation, and technical reinvestment in aging and underdeveloped oil systems globally. For refiners, particularly in the U.S., value is driven by crude quality mismatches, constrained refining capacity, and the ability to process heavier and more complex barrels.

Recent geopolitical developments in Venezuela have brought these dynamics into sharper focus. Political changes and increased U.S. engagement with the country’s energy sector have renewed investor attention not because of immediate production gains, but because of the potential for gradual reinvestment and improved heavy-crude availability.

This shift has supported market interest in large oilfield services companies, such as Schlumberger (SLB), Halliburton (HAL), and Baker Hughes (BKR), on the view that any recovery would be services-led. At the same time, it has reinforced the strategic position of sophisticated refiners that benefit from access to heavy crude and tight refining capacity.

Top Oil Companies to Watch

As global energy demand remains resilient, investment is increasingly focused on maintaining and optimizing existing production and infrastructure. Oilfield services companies and refiners are positioned to benefit from long-term spending needs and the growing complexity of global energy systems.

1. Schlumberger

Schlumberger is the biggest oilfield services company in the world. It works heavily on international and offshore projects. Its strength comes from advanced technology, digital services, and reservoir knowledge.

Energy producers depend on these to get the most from existing fields. Global production is shifting to more complex reservoirs. Schlumberger will benefit from the demand for valuable, technology-driven services.

2. Halliburton

Halliburton is a leading provider of drilling and completion services, with particular strength in North American shale. The company tends to benefit early in upcycles as producers increase activity and service intensity. Halliburton focuses on efficiency and execution. This supports profit margins, even with more careful growth in the industry.

3. Baker Hughes

Baker Hughes operates at the intersection of traditional oil services and energy technology. The company is well-known for its work in liquefied natural gas infrastructure, turbines, and industrial energy equipment, along with its main oilfield services business.

This diversified exposure allows Baker Hughes to invest in oil and gas now while also positioning for longer-term changes in the global energy system.

4. Phillips 66 (PSX)

Phillips 66 is a leading downstream energy company with a strong focus on refining, midstream, and chemicals. The company benefits from U.S. refining capacity constraints and strong product demand, particularly for transportation fuels. Its diversified income and careful spending have made it a steady producer of cash flow, even with changing oil prices.

5. Valero (VLO)

Valero is one of the largest independent refiners in the world, with a strong focus on the U.S. Gulf Coast. The company gains from advanced refineries that can process cheaper heavy and sour crude. This helps maintain strong profits when feedstock prices vary. This puts Valero in a good position, especially in a time of limited refining capacity.

What 3 Things to Consider When Investing in Oil

1. Inflation Protection and Global Demand

Oil services companies have historically performed well during inflationary periods when higher energy prices translate into increased activity and earnings. These companies may also benefit from global energy demand in the long run. Emerging economies continue to grow and need reliable power sources.

2. Volatility and Structural Risks

Oil is a cyclical industry, and price swings can be significant. Periods of oversupply, economic slowdowns, or sharp corrections in oil prices can weigh heavily on returns. Longer term, the global shift toward sustainable energy introduces uncertainty, even as many traditional energy companies adapt their business models.

3. Portfolio Role and Implementation

Exposure to oilfield services and refiners is often best used as a modest allocation within a diversified portfolio. It can complement core holdings by adding diversification and inflation sensitivity. Investors can also use strategies like the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) to express shorter-term views, benefiting from liquidity, low costs, and straightforward tax treatment without K-1 forms.

How to Invest in Oil

For investors looking for targeted exposure to oilfield services, one approach is through a diversified ETF rather than individual stocks. The VanEck Oil Services ETF is designed to track the performance of U.S.-listed oil services companies that support upstream oil and gas production, including drilling, equipment, and related services.

OIH focuses on highly liquid, industry-leading companies, with an index methodology that emphasizes market capitalization and trading volume. This results in exposure to the largest and most established players in the oil services space. The fund may also include U.S.-listed foreign companies, providing broader industry representation while maintaining liquidity and transparency.

As a result, OIH can be a main choice for investors wanting longer-term exposure to oilfield services or as a tactical tool to express a view on rising global energy investment, without the complexity of owning individual stocks or commodity-linked structures.

Investors seeking refining exposure may also consider ETFs focused on downstream companies, such as the VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK), which targets firms benefiting from refining margins, capacity constraints, and crude quality dynamics.

Important Disclosures

Schlumberger (SLB) comprised 21.71% of OIH’s NAV as of 1/16/2026

Halliburton (HAL) comprised 7.87% of OIH’s NAV as of 1/16/2026

Baker Hughes (BKR) comprised 12.36% of OIH’s NAV as of 1/16/2026

Phillips 66 (PSX) comprised 7.57% of CRAK’s NAV as of 1/16/2026

Valero (VLO) comprised 6.66% of CRAK’s as of 1/16/2026

This is not an offer to buy or sell, or a recommendation to buy or sell any of the securities, financial instruments or digital assets mentioned herein. The information presented does not involve the rendering of personalized investment, financial, legal, tax advice, or any call to action. Certain statements contained herein may constitute projections, forecasts and other forward-looking statements, which do not reflect actual results, are for illustrative purposes only, are valid as of the date of this communication, and are subject to change without notice. Actual future performance of any assets or industries mentioned are unknown. Information provided by third party sources are believed to be reliable and have not been independently verified for accuracy or completeness and cannot be guaranteed. VanEck does not guarantee the accuracy of third party data. The information herein represents the opinion of the author(s), but not necessarily those of VanEck or its other employees.

MVIS Global Oil Refiners Index is the exclusive property of MarketVector Indexes GmbH (a wholly owned subsidiary of Van Eck Securities Corporation), which has contracted with Solactive AG to maintain and calculate the Index. The Oil Services Index is the exclusive property of MVIS (a wholly owned subsidiary of the Adviser), which has contracted with Solactive AG to maintain and calculate the Oil Services Index. Solactive AG uses its best efforts to ensure that the Oil Services Index is calculated correctly. Irrespective of its obligations towards MVIS, Solactive AG has no obligation to point out errors in the Oil Services Index to third parties. VanEck Vectors Oil Services ETF is not sponsored, endorsed, sold or promoted by MVIS and MVIS makes no representation regarding the advisability of investing in the VanEck Vectors Oil Services ETF.

An investment in the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) may be subject to risks which include, among others, investing oil services companies depositary receipts, energy sector, small- and medium-capitalization companies, equity securities, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount risk and liquidity of fund shares, issuer-specific changes, non-diversified and concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Small- and medium-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks.

An investment in the VanEck Oil Refiners ETF (CRAK) may be subject to risks which include, but are not limited to, risks related to investments in oil refining companies, special risk considerations of investing in Asian, European and Japanese issuers, foreign securities, emerging market issuers, foreign currency, depositary receipts, energy sector, equity securities, medium-capitalization companies, cash transactions, market, operational, index tracking, authorized participant concentration, no guarantee of active trading market, trading issues, passive management, fund shares trading, premium/discount risk and liquidity of fund shares, issuer-specific changes, non-diversified and index-related concentration risks, all of which may adversely affect the Fund. Emerging market issuers and foreign securities may be subject to securities markets, political and economic, investment and repatriation restrictions, different rules and regulations, less publicly available financial information, foreign currency and exchange rates, operational and settlement, and corporate and securities laws risks. Medium-capitalization companies may be subject to elevated risks.

Investing involves substantial risk and high volatility, including possible loss of principal. An investor should consider the investment objective, risks, charges and expenses of the Fund carefully before investing. To obtain a prospectus and summary prospectus, which contains this and other information, call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com/etfs. Please read the prospectus and summary prospectus carefully before investing.
