data.title: Teledyne: Re-Rated As Belief In Long-Term Compounding Returns
data.description:
Shares of Teledyne Technologies (TDY) have risen sharply to fresh highs, as improved fundamentals of the business are filtering through in the business performance and its outlook.

In fact, some of this momentum was seen arriving late in 2024 already, when I last looked at shares of Teledyne, this was a $480 stock at the time.

Teledyne is a great long-term value creator but stepped away from its typical bolt-on M&A strategy to create shareholder value with a much larger deal for FLIR. This was followed by some underwhelming recent operating performance, making some investors question if the long-term value creation model was broken. With solid operating momentum returning, shares have rightfully commanded a premium again, leaving no immediate opportunities.

About Teledyne - Everywhere You Look

Teledyne develops technology that enables sensing, analyzing, and distribution of information, the business describing itself as a global sensing and decision-support company. The company claims to have a broad and diversified business, consisting of highly engineered products. The company is a real shareholder value creator, driven by these operating capabilities being combined with solid capital allocation decisions.

The company generates some $6.1 billion in annual sales, with results reported across four segments. The largest of these is digital imaging, a segment representing half of sales, as segment margins are seen in the mid- to high double-digits. Products cover all wavelengths of the spectrum with this segment carefully crafted by numerous acquisitions over the past decades.

Instrumentation constitutes a quarter of sales, these being very lucrative sales with margins reported in the high twenties. Of interest is a rapidly growing and just over $1 billion aerospace & defense business, although that growth has been aided by M&A here as well. These are very lucrative activities with margins reported in the twenties. All this is complemented by smaller, lumpier, and lower-margin +$400 million engineered systems.

About half of sales are generated in the US, split pretty evenly between commercial and government markets, complemented by European and Asian activities, predominantly.

The company has increased sales tenfold over the past quarter, aided by more than 70 separate M&A deals, combined reported at a +$12 billion cost. Many of these were smaller deals, but the outlier was a much larger deal for FLIR at an $8 billion price tag, as average M&A deals typically run in the tens of millions, often hundreds of millions at best.

Besides growing sales, EBITDA margins have improved from low single digits to levels in the mid-twenties, only adding to the operating performance demonstrated upon, with earnings having risen from less than a dollar to about $20 per share.

Back To The Fall Of 2024

Following the integration of FLIR, the company was dealing with a tougher environment, this being the environment in which inflation was high in a post-pandemic world. This made it so that 2023 sales rose by just 3%, as growth was seen up to 4% in 2024, although aided by its bolt-on M&A playbook. While this growth looks solid, it felt soft given the past performance of Teledyne and its track record.

However, shares have risen to a high around $480 per share by the fall, after revenues were actually down in the first half of 2024, causing pressure on both the sales and earnings guidance. However, topline sales growth returned to positive 3% in the third quarter, following some orders being booked, as earnings were seen around $19.35 per share for 2024. A net debt load of $2.2 billion worked down to a 2 times leverage ratio, valuing equity at $23 billion and the enterprise at $25 billion.

Besides some bolt-on dealmaking, a larger $710 million deal to acquire the aerospace & defense electronics business from Excelitas was announced in the fall of 2024. With earnings seen near $20 per share while shares traded near the $500 mark, earnings multiples had risen to 24-25 times earnings, while leverage was seen ticking up quite a bit towards 3 times, as the organic performance felt a bit soft.

Rocketing Higher

Shares of Teledyne ended up falling victim to the trade tariff talks in the spring of last year, falling to the lower $400s. However, shares rose to the near $600 mark in the fall of that year, corrected to the $500 mark by year-end, and now have rallied to $620 again as investors embraced the fourth quarter results being released.

After ending 2024 on a stronger note, full-year adjusted earnings ended up increasing four cents to $19.73 per share, aided by M&A efforts and growth. The company guided for 2025 earnings to advance to $21.10-$21.50 per share.

After posting an impressive more than 7% increase in first quarter sales, the company announced another bolt-on deal, as the company acquired Maretron assets from Littlefuse (LFUS), with few details announced. Second quarter sales ended up increasing more than 10%, as later that summer the company announced the acquisition of the TransponderTech business from Saab, with no details announced at the time. After third-quarter sales rose by almost 7%, Teledyne started 2026 with another bolt-on deal, this time acquiring UK-based DD-Scientific.

Hitting New highs

Teledyne just announced strong results for the end of 2025, causing shares to jump 10% to $620 per share. Fourth quarter sales rose by more than 7%, with full-year sales up nearly 8% to $6.12 billion. Acquisitions contributed about $270 million of this growth, or just over 4%, with reported growth split pretty evenly between organic and inorganic achievements.

The company leveraged all this pretty well, with adjusted earnings up some 11% to $21.99 per share, as M&A is seen continuing to contribute to 2026 growth. This came after M&A picked up in 2025, with spending on acquisitions reported at $821 million. Net debt is reported at $2.1 billion, as I peg EBITDA just over $1.4 billion, for a leverage ratio around 1.5 times, as Teledyne itself even reports a 1.4 times leverage ratio.

The company guided for continued growth this year, with earnings seen advancing to $23.65 per share, plus or minus twenty cents. Earnings growth is seen outpacing sales growth, with sales seen moving up just about 4% to $6.37 billion, which feels a bit soft amidst incremental M&A activity, certainly amidst a 1.08 times book-to-bill ratio reported in 2025.

Given this momentum and a solid outlook, the business has found itself on a stronger footing, with the company returning to mid-single-digit organic growth rates. This is accompanied by bolt-on M&A, which the company beautifully calls ¨string of pearl¨ acquisition strategy, driving growth.

This, however, has pushed up earnings multiples to 28 times earnings based on 2025 earnings and 26 times forward earnings, as the adjusted earnings numbers look quite clean, in all fairness.

Given the great track record and improved momentum, I understand why some multiple expansion has been seen. With a net earnings yield just near 4%, investors are betting heavily on growth (with no dividends paid), making me a bit reserved. This also follows shares having risen just 25% in the time frame of a quarter. However, with renewed momentum seen in the business, I am eager to revisit the thesis on dips here.

data.title: Palantir's Revenue Surge: Don't Let P/E Ratios Fool You
data.description:
When I last covered Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) on October 14th, 2025 (with a “buy” rating) in my article “Palantir: $1 Billion Quarter Changes Everything”, the stock was beginning to lose some of the bullish momentum that had characterized share price trends during the prior six-month period. Given the massive, longer-term rallies that this stock has managed to generate, it wasn’t completely surprising to see that some analysts were suggesting that it might be a good time to start taking profits. However, in my article, I argued that I was maintaining my positive outlook because the customer count and revenue growth rate in Palantir’s U.S. commercial segment would probably continue driving the stock higher into the end of 2025.

Unfortunately, this did not turn out to be the case because PLTR shares are actually slightly lower now (despite the small gains of 5.1% that have been provided by the S&P 500 during the same period of time). At this stage, I think investors can look at this activity in one of two ways. First, and more negatively, it can be argued that the stock’s upward momentum is gone and that the “gravity” of overextended valuations is ready to take over. Second, more enthusiastic investors might argue that the stock is simply basing for the next run higher. I will explain why I believe the positive side of the argument appears to be stronger, and I will outline potential price levels that will indicate a reversal point in the long-term uptrend if my bullish thesis turns out to be incorrect.

Palantir’s prior earnings results were quite strong, with beats on both the top and bottom lines. The total revenue figure indicated YoY growth of 63% and surpassed $1 billion once again. Net income results were even more impressive (at $475.6 million), which implied astronomical YoY gains of more than 231%. Revenue from the commercial business improved by more than 2x (at $397 million), and contract values for closed deals in the segment saw an expansion greater than 4x (at $1.31 billion). These figures were released not long after new partnerships with Lumen Technologies, Inc. (LUMN), NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), and Snowflake Inc. (SNOW) were announced, so I would say that it’s somewhat surprising that all of this activity failed to ignite a surge in share prices.

The reality is that the stock has essentially flatlined since then, even though full-year sales guidance has been raised to $4.4 billion with $1.9 - 2.1 billion in free cash flow now expected for the period. In some sense, this should force PLTR investors to take a step back and question what exactly is going on here, because when a stock fails to produce substantive gains when a wide swath of positive information is released, what is going to occur if the next earnings report shows evidence of weakness? Palantir’s valuation issues are the most likely explanation for the stock’s inability to move higher into the end of 2025. But I disagree (at least to some extent) with the metrics that are typically cited when having these types of discussions.

I’ll start with a metric that doesn’t seem to get very much attention, which is Palantir’s forward PEG ratio. Rather than simply looking at the stock’s nosebleed-level P/E ratio, PLTR does not look anywhere near as overvalued when we add a growth component to the equation. Not only is Palantir’s PEG ratio below the 1.0 threshold (which, on its own, suggests that expectations for earnings gains are enough to justify an elevated P/E), but the stock is also trading near the mid-point of the peer group. On one hand, we can see that CrowdStrike Holdings, Inc. (CRWD) and Okta, Inc. (OKTA) might be better positioned at the moment. But Fortinet, Inc. (FTNT) is much more expensive, and Palo Alto Networks, Inc. (PANW) is trading in roughly similar territory. On balance, Palantir is still considered to be roughly in line with industry averages, and this shows that a simple look at P/E alone is not enough to fairly analyze PLTR.

Palantir’s trend in absolute free cash flow is also improving, with current numbers that are now printing toward the top of the peer group. Indeed, Fortinet is still looking stronger in this regard. But I am much more concerned with the underlying trend than I am with the absolute figures. As long as Palantir can continue showing expansion in free cash flow, it will remain clear that the company is scaling into a major cash generator. This is important because expansion of cash flow is a factor that can compress valuation multiples—even if share prices do not see substantial declines. If we are going to expect PLTR to maintain its long-term uptrend, I think this might be one of the most critical metrics to watch because it can still justify the bullish momentum.

Now, on the negative side, I do need to mention that Palantir’s free cash flow yield is an obvious risk because it is the lowest in this industry grouping. What this tells us is that the market has already priced in most of the company’s expected cash flow figure. To be sure, this is quite an unforgiving metric, and it implies that even the slightest miss in quarterly performance can cause a dramatic re-rating in the share price.

To settle this debate, I would argue that investors should finally consider the differences between Palantir’s trailing and forward price-sales ratios. Here, there is a wide gap between the forward and TTM figures (roughly 65x vs. 112x). Interestingly, this forward compression essentially proves that the market expects to see significant improvements in the underlying sales trends. If share prices continue trading with this flat trajectory (as we saw into the end of 2025), Palantir’s “elevated” multiples can compress by themselves. Moreover, forward multiples that are significantly lower will typically suggest that a business is still working through a hyper-growth phase that the market expects to continue.

Of course, none of this is a guarantee, and with a stock like Palantir (that is “priced to perfection”), there is always the potential for quarterly reports to offer an injection of extreme volatility. The next earnings release is scheduled for February 2nd, with consensus estimates for GAAP EPS calling for $0.18 on $1.34 billion in revenue. Normally, I would not place as much emphasis on analyst expectations for the top-line and bottom-line figures. But when we are dealing with a stock that has a heavy (and very loyal) retail following and share price gains of more than 2,200% in less than three years, it should be understood that wild and volatile price swings can occur if quarterly results fail to match expectations.

Personally, I will be watching for changes in free cash flow expectations because this is the metric that I am using as a basis for large portions of my total outlook. In terms of real share price action, critical support zones have developed near $148.20 (which marks the lows from November 21st, 2025, and the Neckline of a potential Head and Shoulders pattern). There is also a confluence here with the 200-Day exponential moving average, so I think that any downside break of this area could signal a longer-term reversal in the bullish trend. Of course, that area has not yet broken, and a Head and Shoulders pattern is not actually “triggered” unless there is a downside breach of the neckline.

Conversely, positive surprises in the upcoming earnings report should help to keep this support zone intact. If this does turn out to be true, the next major area of resistance comes into play near $207.50, which marks the highs from November 3rd, 2025. As a result, I do think that PLTR stock is probably trading near an inflection point. Sideways trading activity tends to characterize periods of indecision, so there is a lot riding on the next quarterly release. From a trading perspective, I would let the charts do most of the work because these aforementioned price levels are likely to work as major indicators of whether the long-term uptrend actually has the potential to continue through the first half of 2026.
